title: Share M-extension arithmetic datapaths
motivation: The current combinational ALU builds three independent 64-bit multiplier paths and separate signed/unsigned divide and remainder operators. On a small FPGA this likely consumes LUTs and routing near the design's current limit, depressing Fmax even though the core only ever selects one M-extension result per instruction.

title: Add an instruction fetch FIFO
motivation: CoreMark fitness is measured with roughly 22% random instruction-memory stalls, and the current IF stage injects a bubble into decode on every stalled fetch. A shallow fetch FIFO can use ready cycles to prefetch sequential instructions and let decode keep running through isolated imem stalls, improving iterations per cycle while keeping the existing single-issue retirement model.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Restructure fetch into a small sequential instruction FIFO with a separate fetch PC and decode pop path. When imemReady is high and the FIFO is not full, enqueue the fetched instruction and advance the fetch PC; when decode is not stalled, pop one entry into ID. Redirects flush the FIFO and restart fetch at the redirect target, while dmem/load-use stalls simply stop popping so prefetched instructions are retained. This should hide many one-cycle imem bubbles in the apples-to-apples random-stall CoreMark mode with modest LUT cost and minimal impact on the execute/data-memory critical paths.

title: Add static backward-taken branch prediction
motivation: The current five-stage core always fetches pc+4 and only redirects after EX resolves a taken branch or jump, so every taken CoreMark loop branch loses the younger IF/ID and ID/EX work. CoreMark's hot loops are dominated by short backward conditional branches, making a small static predictor a good fit for improving iterations/cycle without spending many LUTs or adding a timing-heavy dynamic table.

title: Gate false load-use stalls
motivation: The baseline load-use interlock treats both rs1 and rs2 bitfields as live operands for every following instruction. For OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and SYSTEM encodings, one or both of those fields are immediates or unused, so a preceding load can inject a bubble even when the next instruction has no true dependency. CoreMark has dense load/immediate/control sequences, so eliminating these false bubbles should improve iterations per cycle with only a small amount of decode logic.

title: Add one-entry store drain buffer
motivation: The baseline freezes the entire pipeline whenever a store reaches MEM while dmemReady is low. In the CoreMark fitness mode dmem stalls are random and frequent enough that independent arithmetic behind ordinary RAM stores can be blocked even though the store data and address are already known. The prior structural IF FIFO regressed by adding front-end control and redirect cost; a store drain buffer targets the back end instead and can hide store-only wait states without touching fetch prediction.

title: Make DIV/REM iterative
motivation: The current execute stage includes synthesized single-cycle DIV/REM operators in the combinational ALU, so rare divider instructions can consume LUTs and routing on the timing path used by every integer instruction. title: Share RV32M multiply hardware
motivation: After DIV/REM moved into an iterative unit, the largest always-present arithmetic cost left in the EX-stage ALU is the multiply family. The current ALU computes three separate 64-bit products for signed-signed, unsigned-unsigned, and signed-unsigned forms even though each instruction selects only one result. Prior M-extension sharing regressed because it kept division on a long combinational path; this proposal narrows the optimization to multiplier duplication only.

title: Add load-stall ALU skid
motivation: The current best core has already removed DIV/REM from the EX critical path and added a one-entry store drain buffer, but cycles are still essentially set by the original in-order pipeline plus random bus stalls. Loads remain fully blocking: when an ordinary RAM load waits for dmemReady, the whole front end and EX stage freeze even if the next instruction is an independent simple ALU operation whose operands are already available. A small back-end completion skid targets that remaining dmem-stall exposure without adding predictor state or new fetch-side timing paths, which prior attempts showed are expensive on this FPGA.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a one-entry in-order completion skid for one younger, independent, non-memory, non-control integer instruction behind a stalled ordinary RAM load. When EX/MEM holds an aligned RAM load with dmemReady low, and ID/EX contains a legal simple ALU/LUI/AUIPC instruction that does not read the load destination, execute that younger instruction once, capture its full RVFI/MEM-WB payload in a side register, and then stop issue while the skid is full. When the load finally reaches MEM/WB, retire the load first on RVFI channel 0, then drain the skidded ALU completion on the following cycle before accepting newer EX work; channel 1 remains tied off. Loads, stores, branches, jumps, fences, SYSTEM instructions, illegal instructions, MMIO accesses, and any possible RAW dependence on the pending load keep the existing precise stall behavior. This should hide isolated random load wait cycles in CoreMark while preserving strict in-order retirement, memory ordering, trap behavior, and the top-level NRET=2 contract.

title: Predict direct JAL in IF
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts lost more FPGA timing than they recovered in branch
penalty: decode-stage backward prediction added a long immediate-to-PC path,
and even tiny BTB/loop tables added enough PC compare, target mux, and
prediction metadata to regress. The remaining control-flow predictor angle
worth testing is narrower: exact direct-JAL next-PC prediction, which has no
aliasing, no counters, and no conditional outcome speculation.

motivation: The largest recent structural win came from removing DIV/REM from the always-active EX critical path, and the follow-up unsigned-product sharing win suggests RV32M multiply hardware is still relevant to placement and timing. title: Add tiny RAS return predictor
motivation: The current core still pays the full EX-stage redirect penalty for every JALR return. Prior predictor attempts regressed because they added branch-immediate adders, BTB tag/counter lookup, or loop-target compares to the fetch path. CoreMark should still execute repeated call/return control flow inside the timed workload, so a return-only predictor is the remaining predictor niche with a materially smaller timing footprint than the failed branch/JAL predictors.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a tiny 2-entry return-address stack that predicts only canonical returns, such as JALR x0, 0(x1) or JALR x0, 0(x5), when the stack is non-empty. Calls that write x1/x5 push pc+4 when they resolve in EX, and resolved returns pop in EX; IF uses only a simple return-instruction bit check plus the stack-top target to choose the next PC. title: Drop regfile reset fanout
motivation:
The current register file clears all 32 integer registers with a reset-time
loop. That likely prevents clean FPGA memory inference for the 32x32 GPR
array and creates a wide reset/control fanout for state that RV32I does not
architecturally require to be zero after reset. Recent successful hypotheses
improved fitness by removing always-present timing or routing pressure
without adding CPI, so this is a better fit than another predictor, skid, or
M-extension sidecar.

added target/tag state and next-PC muxing; a plain IF/ID register is a narrower
structural retiming that attacks the fetch-to-decode timing path without adding
prediction policy or a multi-entry queue.

title: Simplify RAM store range decode
motivation:
The current store-buffer path decides whether a store is ordinary RAM by
comparing the full 32-bit effective address against the power-of-two

hazard classification and the MEM-stage buffer enqueue path, so it can add
carry-chain logic and routing on a timing-sensitive store-buffer control
path even though the predicate is just "upper address bits are zero".
